The council has its headquarters at [County Hall](/wiki/County_Hall,_Durham "County Hall, Durham") in the city of [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"), and consists of 98 councillors. It is a member of the [North East Combined Authority](/wiki/North_East_Combined_Authority "North East Combined Authority").

**Durham County Council** is the [local authority](/wiki/Local_government_in_England "Local government in England") for the [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England") area of [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham_(district) "County Durham (district)") in North East England. The unitary authority area is smaller than the [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_county "Ceremonial county") of [Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), which additionally includes [Darlington](/wiki/Borough_of_Darlington "Borough of Darlington"), [Hartlepool](/wiki/Borough_of_Hartlepool "Borough of Hartlepool"), and part of [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees"). The council has its headquarters at [County Hall](/wiki/County_Hall,_Durham "County Hall, Durham") in the city of [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"), and consists of 98 councillors. It is a member of the [North East Combined Authority](/wiki/North_East_Combined_Authority "North East Combined Authority").

Since the [2025 Durham County Council election](/wiki/2025_Durham_County_Council_election "2025 Durham County Council election") the council has been under the majority control of [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK"). The chair of the council is Robbie Rodis, and the leader is Andrew Husband. The council had a [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") majority from 1925 until 2021, when it fell into [no overall control](/wiki/No_overall_control "No overall control").

Between 1889 and 1974 Durham consisted of an [administrative county](/wiki/Administrative_county "Administrative county") governed by a county council, and several [county boroughs](/wiki/County_borough "County borough") with their own councils. In 1974, as part of [reforms to local government in England](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), Durham was reconstituted as a two-tier [non-metropolitan county](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") governed by a county council and eight district councils. In 1997, the [Borough of Darlington](/wiki/Borough_of_Darlington "Borough of Darlington") was reconstituted as a unitary authority, making it independent of the county council. Durham County Council was itself reconstituted as a unitary authority in 2009, when the seven remaining district councils were abolished and the county council took on their responsibilities.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_County_Council&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Elected county councils were established in 1889 under the [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888"), taking over administrative functions previously carried out by unelected [magistrates](/wiki/Magistrate "Magistrate") at the [quarter sessions](/wiki/Quarter_sessions "Quarter sessions"). The boroughs of [Gateshead](/wiki/Gateshead "Gateshead"), [South Shields](/wiki/South_Shields "South Shields") and [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland") were considered large enough to provide their own county-level services and so they were made [county boroughs](/wiki/County_borough "County borough"), independent from Durham County Council. The county council was elected by and provided services to the rest of the county, which area was termed the [administrative county](/wiki/Administrative_county "Administrative county").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Additional county boroughs were later created at [West Hartlepool](/wiki/West_Hartlepool "West Hartlepool") in 1902 and [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington") in 1915. In 1967 West Hartlepool merged with the neighbouring borough of [Hartlepool](/wiki/Hartlepool "Hartlepool") (which had just covered the [old town](/wiki/Headland,_Hartlepool "Headland, Hartlepool")), with the enlarged county borough thereafter being called Hartlepool. [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees"), [Billingham](/wiki/Billingham "Billingham") and surrounding areas were removed from the administrative county in 1968 to become part of the [County Borough of Teesside](/wiki/County_Borough_of_Teesside "County Borough of Teesside").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Durham_Crown_Court%2C_December_2020.jpg/250px-Durham_Crown_Court%2C_December_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Crown_Court,_December_2020.jpg)

Durham Crown Court, formerly Shire Hall: Council's first meeting place 1889–1898

The first elections took place in January 1889 and the county council formally came into being on 1 April 1889. On that day its first official meeting was held at the old [Shire Hall](/wiki/Durham_Crown_Court "Durham Crown Court") on Old Elvet in Durham, the courthouse (built 1811) which had served as the meeting place of the quarter sessions which preceded the county council.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The first chairman of the council was [John Lloyd Wharton](/wiki/John_Lloyd_Wharton "John Lloyd Wharton"), who was the [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament "Member of Parliament") for [Ripon](/wiki/Ripon_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Ripon (UK Parliament constituency)") (in [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire")); he had also been chairman of the Durham Quarter Sessions since 1871.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Durham was the first county council to be controlled by the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), which won the most seats in 1919.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

In 1974, the county was redesignated as a non-metropolitan county under the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"). As part of those reforms the county ceded territory in the north-east to the new county of [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear") and in the south-east to the new county of [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland_(county) "Cleveland (county)"), but gained the former [Startforth Rural District](/wiki/Startforth_Rural_District "Startforth Rural District") covering the part of [Teesdale](/wiki/Teesdale "Teesdale") south of the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees") from the [North Riding of Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Riding_of_Yorkshire "North Riding of Yorkshire"), and Darlington was brought back under the county council's control.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Until 1974, the lower tier of local government comprised numerous [boroughs](/wiki/Municipal_borough "Municipal borough"), [urban districts](/wiki/Urban_district_(England_and_Wales) "Urban district (England and Wales)") and [rural districts](/wiki/Rural_districts "Rural districts"). The districts were also reorganised in 1974 into eight [non-metropolitan districts](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district"): [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street_(district) "Chester-le-Street (district)"), [Darlington](/wiki/Borough_of_Darlington "Borough of Darlington"), [Derwentside](/wiki/Derwentside "Derwentside"), [Durham](/wiki/Durham_(non-metropolitan_district) "Durham (non-metropolitan district)"), [Easington](/wiki/Easington_(district) "Easington (district)"), [Sedgefield](/wiki/Sedgefield_(borough) "Sedgefield (borough)"), [Teesdale](/wiki/Teesdale_(district) "Teesdale (district)"), and [Wear Valley](/wiki/Wear_Valley "Wear Valley").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In 1997, Darlington became a unitary authority, removing it from county council control.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Durham County Council itself became a unitary authority on 1 April 2009, when the seven remaining non-metropolitan districts of the county were abolished and the county council absorbed their functions.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The legislation which made the county council a unitary authority allowed the council to omit the word 'County' from its name to become 'Durham Council', but in the event the name 'Durham County Council' was kept.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[a]](#cite_note-16)

In 2024 a [combined authority](/wiki/Combined_authority "Combined authority") was established covering Durham, [Gateshead](/wiki/Borough_of_Gateshead "Borough of Gateshead"), [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"), [North Tyneside](/wiki/North_Tyneside "North Tyneside"), [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), [South Tyneside](/wiki/South_Tyneside "South Tyneside") and [Sunderland](/wiki/City_of_Sunderland "City of Sunderland"), called the [North East Mayoral Combined Authority](/wiki/North_East_Mayoral_Combined_Authority "North East Mayoral Combined Authority"). It is chaired by the directly elected [Mayor of the North East](/wiki/Mayor_of_the_North_East "Mayor of the North East") and oversees the delivery of certain strategic functions across the area.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_County_Council&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Governance")]

Since 2009, Durham County Council has provided both [county-level](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county") and [district-level](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district") services. Much of the county is also covered by [civil parishes](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish"), which form a lower tier of local government for their areas.[[17]](#cite_note-electionmaps-18)

### Political control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_County_Council&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Political control")]

The council has been under [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK") majority control since the [2025 election](/wiki/2025_Durham_County_Council_election "2025 Durham County Council election").[[18]](#cite_note-19)

Durham was the first county council to be controlled by [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), who took power in 1919. Between 1922 and 1925, the council was under no overall control with a Labour minority administration. From 1925 until 2021, Labour held a majority. Political control since 1919 has been as follows:[[19]](#cite_note-Results-20)[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[20]](#cite_note-compositions-21)

### Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_County_Council&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Composition")]

Following the [2025 election](/wiki/2025_Durham_County_Council_election "2025 Durham County Council election"), and subsequent changes up to June 2025, the composition of the council was:[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33)

| Party | | Councillors |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK") | 62 |
|  | [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)") | 15 |
|  | [Independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") | 12 |
|  | [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") | 5 |
|  | [Green](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") | 2 |
|  | [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") | 1 |
|  | [Independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") | 1 |
|  | [Vacant](/wiki/Casual_vacancy "Casual vacancy") | 0 |
| Total | | 98 |

Of the twelve independent councillors, seven sit with the Green Party as the "Durham County Council Independent Group", two form the "Spennymoor and Tudhoe Independent Group", and the other three are not aligned to any group. After two Reform UK councillors stepped down, one of the seats was taken by the Lib Dems in a by-election in July.[[33]](#cite_note-34) The other seat remained vacant until a by-election on 7 August[[34]](#cite_note-35) that was won by Reform UK.[[35]](#cite_note-36) The next full council elections are due in 2029.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

## Elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_County_Council&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Elections")]

See also: [Durham County Council elections](/wiki/Durham_County_Council_elections "Durham County Council elections")

From the previous boundary changes in 2013 the council comprised 126 [councillors](/wiki/Councillor "Councillor") representing 63 [electoral divisions](/wiki/Wards_and_electoral_divisions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Wards and electoral divisions of the United Kingdom"), with each division electing one, two or three councillors. Elections are held every four years.[[37]](#cite_note-38) New division boundaries were drawn up to take effect from the 2025 election, reducing the number of councillors to 98.[[38]](#cite_note-39)

## Premises

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Durham_County_Council&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Premises")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Indigo_Hotel_%28Shire_Hall%29%2C_Durham%2C_December_2020.jpg/250px-Indigo_Hotel_%28Shire_Hall%29%2C_Durham%2C_December_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indigo_Hotel_(Shire_Hall),_Durham,_December_2020.jpg)

[Shire Hall](/wiki/Shire_Hall,_Durham "Shire Hall, Durham"), Old Elvet: Council's headquarters 1898–1963

The council is based at [County Hall](/wiki/County_Hall,_Durham "County Hall, Durham") at Aykley Heads in the northern suburbs of the city of Durham. The building was purpose-built for the council and was completed in 1963.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

When first created the council met at the courthouse on Old Elvet, which at the time was known as Shire Hall. A few years after its creation the council decided to build its own headquarters on a site nearby, also on Old Elvet, which was also given the name [Shire Hall](/wiki/Shire_Hall,_Durham "Shire Hall, Durham"). The new building was completed in 1898, after which the old Shire Hall became known as the Assizes Court, and since 1971 as [Durham Crown Court](/wiki/Durham_Crown_Court "Durham Crown Court").[[40]](#cite_note-41)

The council has announced plans to move to the Rivergreen building, also in the Aykley Heads area of Durham, in 2025, with the intention that County Hall would then be redeveloped.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** Like most unitary authorities, the way County Durham was legally made a unitary authority was by creating both a county and a district which cover the same area and then directing that only one of them should have a council, which performs both district and county functions. Unusually, the county and district have different names in this case: the non-metropolitan county (which had been created and named in the Local Government Act 1972) is called 'Durham', the non-metropolitan district created in the 2009 reforms is called 'County Durham'. The district does not have its own council, but the county council has been given district-level functions in addition to the county-level functions it already had.
